title: Mirza ghasemi
subtitle: Iranian smoked aubergine with eggs and tomato
excerpt: Aubergines charred directly over a flame until completely blackened and smoky, then scooped from their skins and cooked down with garlic, tomato, turmeric, and eggs scrambled through the smoky mixture. A northern Iranian speciality from Gilan province, related to but distinct from Middle Eastern mutabal, here the aubergine is cooked rather than served raw.
sourceNotes: Mirza ghasemi is a speciality of Gilan province on the Caspian coast of northern Iran, documented in Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) and Sabrina Ghayour's 'Persiana' (2014). It differs from mutabal and baba ganoush in that the aubergine is cooked in a pan with tomato and garlic after charring, then eggs are scrambled through. It is eaten as a side dish, a dip, or a light main.

content.text: The char is non-negotiable. The aubergines must be placed directly on a gas flame or under a very hot grill and cooked until the skin is completely black and the flesh inside is soft and collapsed. The smoke from the burning skin permeates the flesh and is the flavour of the dish. If you char them in the oven at high heat, some smokiness is lost, a gas flame is strongly preferable. The flesh will be grey and wet when scooped out; squeeze out excess liquid through a sieve.

The eggs are the finishing touch. They are beaten and stirred through the cooked aubergine mixture over medium heat until just set but still a little soft, more like a rough scramble than a firm mixture. They bind the dish and add richness without dominating the smoky aubergine flavour. Serve with warm bread and a spoonful of yoghurt.

items.name: Aubergine
items.prepNote: charred directly over flame or under the grill

content.text: Place the {{aubergine}} directly on a gas burner flame at high heat, turning with tongs every 3 to 4 minutes until completely black and the flesh feels very soft when pressed. Transfer to a colander and leave for 10 minutes. Scoop out the flesh, discard the skins, and drain in a sieve, pressing to remove excess liquid. Roughly chop.

Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a wide pan over medium heat. Cook the {{garlic}} for 1 minute until fragrant. Add the {{turmeric}} and cook for 30 seconds. Add the {{tomato}} and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until reduced and jammy. Add the charred aubergine and stir to combine. Cook for 5 minutes. Make 3 small wells in the mixture and pour the beaten {{eggs}} in. Stir gently over medium heat until the eggs are just set, soft and slightly creamy. Season with salt.

items.fix: The open flame char is essential. Under a grill, place the aubergines as close to the element as possible and be prepared for them to take 30 to 40 minutes to char fully
items.cause: Aubergine not charred over direct flame

items.fix: Press the charred flesh firmly in the sieve and squeeze in a cloth to remove moisture. The tomato also needs to cook down fully before adding the aubergine
Reduce the heat to low before adding the beaten eggs. Stir gently and remove from the heat while the eggs are still slightly underdone. They continue setting off the heat
items.cause: Overcooked or pan too hot when eggs added

items.fix: The skin should be completely black and cracking. When the aubergine is ready, it collapses and the flesh pulls away cleanly from the skin. If the skin is still attached, return to the flame for longer
items.symptom: Aubergine skin is difficult to remove

content.text: Mirza ghasemi comes from Gilan, the lush province on Iran's Caspian coast. Gilani cooking is its own tradition, distinct from the rest of Iran. It leans on fresh herbs, pomegranate, fish, and egg dishes. The name is thought to come from a person, Mirza being an honorific title. The dish is one of the best-known Gilani plates outside the province. It shows up in Persian restaurants and cookbooks as a regional speciality. Charred aubergine and egg pair up in dishes across the Middle East and the Mediterranean, like shakshuka and Turkish patlican plates. The turmeric, garlic, and tomato combination is what makes this version distinctly Iranian.
